But two other avenues. Really quick, Audie, two other avenues for proceeding, if they're serious. Number one, go to the states. Every state can entertain lawsuits, even against the federal government. You can have the attorney general or local prosecutor bring a lawsuit. But also Congress can oversee and step in if ICE is behaving in a manner that's not appropriate.

CORNISH: So, right now, ICE related Mexican national deaths in Trump's term, 14 people in ICE custody, three in ICE operations.

Sheinbaum had more or less kind of played nice with Trump when it came to tariffs, right, and when it came to the drug war. Why do you think, Lulu, that she is speaking out now and in such a specific way, using the phrase targeted?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes. This is always a hugely sensitive issue for Mexico. Let's not forget that, you know, Mexico has the largest number of immigrants in the United States, and they share a border. And so, the way that ICE and Border Patrol interact with that population has always been a source of enormous sensitivity and tension. And I think one of the reasons they're doing this is not only because they think they're going to get satisfaction, but it is to be perceived by their own people as doing something, as raising complaints, as trying to actively engage.

Because it's not only that the government is in this very difficult position with trying to appease Trump and work with him while also defending its population, but the people themselves do not like the Trump administration, do not like the way that this is playing out, and want their government to do something.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And so, this is a way of them showing that they are.

CORNISH: And it raises the profile of ICE operations in a moment when they had effectively lowered the temperature after the mass protests in two separate cities, and deaths on the streets. TERRY SCHILLING, PRESIDENT, AMERICAN PRINCIPLES PROJECT: Yes. Well,

look, I -- my message to the president of Mexico, Claudia Sheinbaum, is, game on. You know, you want to sue over this. I guess we'll have to sue over the hundreds of thousands of young men and women in this country that have died from fentanyl overdoses or even the hundreds of people that have had violence inflicted on them that have been raped and sexually assaulted.

You know, Claudia Sheinbaum is one to talk. She was elected in a year where there were over 27 political assassinations in her party. She's literally a hand-picked candidate for the cartels. And again --

CORNISH: You've levied a lot of different things there.

CORNISH: So, I want to hold on because I'm not fact checking you on that level, OK. But I do have this question about, is it fair for a country to say, you're killing our nationals on your street and that this is state sanctioned deaths, and therefore.

SCHILLING: Well, look, I noticed in that chart, in 2023 and 2024 there were -- I think in 2023 it said there were seven deaths by ICE and 11 deaths in 2024. That's when there was like no immigration enforcement at all. And it's only around 50 between these last two years. There it goes, 33 and 19 in two years. That's 52.

SCHILLING: That's when we're actually deporting people and actually enforcing the law. So, it seems like there's some violence embedded into illegal immigration in the first place.

WILLIAMS: I -- well, no, no, no. I -- the only thing I will say to sort of push back on that gently is, and I worked at -- I was a senior official at ICE for five years. You're still talking about deporting 400,000 people a year, even under the low points of any administration's immigration enforcement efforts.

So, the idea that enforcement simply isn't happening, and those are, I believe we were talking about detention centers, people are detained, beds are full. It's just not accurate that no -- that no enforcement was happening.

Now, certainly we can agree, and I think we should, that more enforcement is happening now than happened in 2023. But I -- but I don't think that that -- those numbers are reflective of enforcement simply not happening in detention centers.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And it was --

WILLIAMS: So, a lot of people are detained by the United States.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And I'll also say that they -- (CROSS TALK)

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And I will say that the implication --

SCHILLING: And a lot more people were coming into the United States in those years.

WILLIAMS: Sure. Sure. Sure.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes, but I'll also say that the implication that somehow enforcement is violent and therefore these deaths are somehow acceptable is --

SCHILLING: Well, when --

CORNISH: And not accept -- well, let's pause on this. It's not acceptable, certainly, to the families. And as we started this story with somebody who's lost their father when they weren't even the target of the operation. So, I think the question of, what standard do we hold ourselves to is coming up in this.

I want you guys to stay with me. We're going to turn to another story in the future talking about NASA, because NASA is now recruiting volunteers for a yearlong moon and mars simulation. Voters will be in space-like -- sorry, volunteers will be in space-like conditions, living inside confined habitats and carrying out tasks, just as astronauts would at the start of the year.

CORNISH: An alleged plot by Iran to assassinate President Trump uncovered by Israel. Sources tell CNN they shared the intelligence with the U.S. this week.

I'm bringing in "New York Times" White House correspondent Maggie Haberman. She's co-author of the new bestseller "Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump."

Maggie, thank you so much for being here.

This seems like a good week to have this conversation between the inflection point ending the ceasefire and then this moment of this reporting we're getting now of a potential assassination attempt, information shared by Israel. This is a president who has survived assassination attempts. Does this kind of concern loom large for him?

MAGGIE HABERMAN, WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENT, "THE NEW YORK TIMES": Well, there's no question that among the reasons that the president and his team were really radicalized in the interregnum period before he came back to office, and we write about this in the book, were the assassination attempts that he faced. In particular, he -- Butler happened around the time that someone had been charged with being part of a murder for hire plot involving -- or set up by Iranian officials allegedly. And in his mind, and in the campaign's mind at the time, it all became one thing. And so, there's no question that the fact that this new information about a potential plot is going to loom large for this team. It is going to raise new concerns.

You are already seeing a president who, Audie, does not travel outside the White House that much. Part of it is that, you know, he would tell aides at the beginning of the -- of the term, I'm done campaigning. But part of it are -- is legitimate security concerns.

CORNISH: It's interesting talk -- hearing from you in this moment because he has such a different cast of characters around him compared to term one. How does that affect something like prosecuting this war, where we see like while people are having talks, he says, look, ceasefire is over, and all of a sudden we are in the middle of strikes again.

HABERMAN: So, there's a couple of things, Audie. One is that this government, unlike term one, and one of the things that we write about very clearly is how radically different this term is than the first term. The decisions are being made by a small handful of about a half a dozen advisors. And if someone is not in the room with those advisors in the government, they often have no idea what is going on. And that is generally by design, including Steve Witkoff, who is Trump's envoy for everything, who had been negotiating this ceasefire or some kind of an Iranian deal for some time.

But we also describe how last year Steve Witkoff thought that he was getting close to a deal, and then Trump made some other pronouncement, and it became very clear that foreign policy, as someone said, you know, and we quote them anonymously in the book, is, whatever Trump says at any given moment.

And so, you are seeing that right now with this ceasefire where I think it is -- it is most problematic is for diplomat -- long-term diplomatic conversations, incremental comments matter, mixed signals matter. And that has obviously happened for some time here. This is also a war that the president clearly wants to be done with. And there just is no quick fix. And that is frustrating him.

CORNISH: I want to turn to another issue because immigration has been obviously a defining issue for Trump in both terms, but arguably he's probably made the most gains in this term. Largely, Stephen Miller has looked at as the architect of that. Can you talk to me about the, where we are now, meaning Noem is gone, we were just talking about Mexico complaining about Mexican nationals being targeted. Is this something that the architects around Trump -- that it's by design?

HABERMAN: So, we have seen a lot of fits and starts with immigration efforts and policy because there was such a flood the zone effort. Stephen Miller spent several years preparing for a second Trump term. He looked at all of the levers of government. He had it -- he has an actual plan for what he wanted to do. He is more hard line on immigration than Trump is, although Trump obviously only tends to react and, you know, reel Miller in when there's negative media coverage. And so, you saw that earlier this year with the shooting deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti. And these showy ICE sweeps into cities, these, you know, dramatic

displays that the administration would clip and put out in social media videos, those slowed down, but that doesn't mean that the efforts slowed down. And so, you are going -- you are seeing -- my colleagues have written about this at "The Times," larger sweeps. You are seeing -- they're just broadcast with less vigor and intensity. It calmed the protests down at the time. I don't know where this goes now. But they are trying now -- Stephen Miller had a target of 3,000 arrests a day. That was not doable. Now there is a smaller target, but it's still quite high. And he is very intent on pushing this ahead for as long as he can.

CORNISH: OK, that's Maggie Haberman. Her new book with Jonathan Swan is called "Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump." It's out now.

I also want to turn to this, the ballot for governor in Colorado, it's set. Marine veteran Victor Marx secured the top Republican spot when the race was called last night. Both of his GOP rivals in the race have said they won't support him in the general election, calling him, quote, "a disaster" and a "con man."

VICTOR MARX (R), COLORADO CANDIDATE FOR GOVERNOR: So, if you are frustrated, skeptical or wondering if anything can actually change, I just proved it by winning the primary.

CORNISH: OK, group chat is back.

This is a coda. I'm sorry, Terry, this is a coda.

SCHILLING: You got me (ph).

CORNISH: You and I talked about this in -- last week. And you were like, look, ignore people like this. This was in our Platner discussion.

CORNISH: And you were like, look, this is not a guy that you should be focusing on. He is now the GOP candidate for governor in Colorado.

SCHILLING: In Colorado, in Illinois, in New York, in California.

CORNISH: But also two Republicans in the race.

CORNISH: Sour grapes or them literally being like, this disaster is a con man. SCHILLING: Again -- look, again, I think one of the biggest travesties

is when these blue states become so blue that they're not competitive. And when they're not competitive, you don't get the quality candidates that you need actually to win.

CORNISH: No, no, no, Terry, he raised $3 million. That's not ate (ph) money from Democrats.

SCHILLING: I know. That's not very much for -- but for a governor race --

SCHILLING: You know, you can raise that just by going on a few punchy podcasts. It's really not that much money for even a congressional race at this point.

CORNISH: Right. He claims to have rescued 45,000 abuse victims. He performs prayers to free people of demonic oppression. Claims to have called an airstrike on ISIS. Says he was forced to kill a man at age seven. Who among us?

SCHILLING: Well, look, we got Abigail Spanberger, who's an admitted CIA operative, as the governor of Virginia. I'd like to know what she's done in her career.

CORNISH: From the CIA?

SCHILLING: From the -- the CIA does some pretty gnarly stuff.

CORNISH: And you don't want to know about demonic -- OK. OK.

SCHILLING: No, I definitely want to know about that. I believe in the spiritual world.

CORNISH: I mean, work with me, Terry. Work with me.

Lulu, the reason why I'm talking about this is because candidate quality, a term that we heard from Mitch McConnell.

WILLIAMS: All week. Yes.

CORNISH: You know what I mean? It's been so on the minds of everyone with the Graham Platner thing in the Senate. And just like in Republican circles, in Colorado, the voters want what the voters want.

What have we learned about the limits of that?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: We've learned that demonic possession is a thing, clearly.

SCHILLING: Yes, 100 percent.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: That is -- that is -- that is --

CORNISH: Also teleporting to a Waffle House. A different story. SCHILLING: That's different.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And teleporting to a Waffle House. I mean -- listen, this is a huge issue, I think, for both Republicans and Democrats and what I think any -- or what I hear from both my Democratic and Republican friends who are in the political system in that way is that they say, well, it's because the parties are so weak and we no longer get to decide about quality candidates -- you know, candidate quality. These decisions used to be made in backrooms. And so we could really vet people and put the best candidate forward.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And that is true.

What voters will say is, yay, I'm so glad that this is no longer being done in backrooms.

CORNISH: I know, but how come some people can ride it out and some people can't? Now, Platner is accused of violence. I think that clearly turned out to be a red line. But there's lots of other people dealing with scandals. Ken Paxton in Texas.

CORNISH: Obviously, Marx. I mean his -- one of the people he ran against said she ran a good race and, quote, "for the record, I still haven't killed anyone." It seems as though --

WILLIAMS: That's the bar.

CORNISH: Yes, that's the bar.

CORNISH: What is the bar for voters?

WILLIAMS: You know, I mean, we have to understand that primary voters in any party are the most activist and sort of most partisan of any voters in the country.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: That's why we should have open primaries.

WILLIAMS: That's why we should have open primaries or ranked choice voting, you know, as you have -- as you have in --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Or ranked choice voting.

CORNISH: Oh, they hate -- the parties hate ranked choice voting.

WILLIAMS: Well, but my point -- sure, but all I'm saying is that when the most activist people are the ones casting the votes, you're going to occasionally get stuff like this in --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: It's not only that --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: But to your point, actually, when you have one party rule in a state, the opposition party becomes very radicalized I think. We see that in California with the GOP. We see that, you know, in Democratic politics and sort of right leaning states. And so, I do think that you get like the most hardcore people putting candidates forward. And we are in an era, we are led by a populist, we are led by someone who was a celebrity, who has innumerable sort of scandals, both previous and current, and I think it's opened the door --

CORNISH: Yes, but what we have -- we've learned, while he may be --

CORNISH: While he may be scandal proof, not everyone else is. That's what we have learned.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes. But maybe. But maybe not.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: I mean, I think everyone's trying their game and they're trying to see how far they can push it until voters or the parties or people just say, the establishment says enough is enough.

Rape seems to -- accusations of rape seem to be that line for the Democrats, but that certainly hasn't been the line for Republicans.

SCHILLING: Well, it was the line until it became clear that Platner was actually weakened and was most likely going to lose. The Democrats did not care about Lyndsey Fifield when she made her accusations because she was a Republican.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: I'm -- what I'm trying to -- what I'm --

SCHILLING: So, it's, believe all Democrat women, not believe any Republican women.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: What I'm -- what I'm trying to say is, you have a president here who has many, many allegations. All I'm saying --

SCHILLING: Not credible.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes, well --

CORNISH: It's funny, we are ending the week the way we started it, almost with the exact same discussion is that --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: I'm not arguing with you.

CORNISH: No, no, it's the same beats I hear partisan wise.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: I'm just saying is, what's good for the goose is good for the gander. And I think both Republicans and Democrats need to -- need to actually grapple with this. This isn't like a he said/she said.

CORNISH: We hope to have more news for you next week.

Thank you for being in the group chat. Thank you for waking up with us. A lot of places you can spend your time, and you choose to spend it with us. I really appreciate it.

I'm Audie Cornish, and the headlines are next.

Return to Transcripts main page

Neighbors Report Seeing McConnell Put in Ambulance on Stretcher; Israel Warns U.S. of Iran Plot to Assassinate Trump; Kylie's Meta Collaboration Sparks Backlash. Aired 6-6:30a ET

Aired July 10, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

BRAD SMITH, CNN ANCHOR: People in California, but not in a good way. This peach truck, it lost some of its fruit on the roadway and caused a major traffic jam.

The peaches were eventually pushed to the median, and the lane was thankfully reopened. As if there's not enough that L.A. drivers have to deal with.

That does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS. I'm Brad Smith. CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: All right. We've got new video just into CNN, showing the moment Mitch McConnell was taken to the hospital a month ago. It's bringing up questions about transparency surrounding his health.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: He's obviously not doing well, but don't know if he's alive or has passed away.

CORNISH: And Israel shares intel of an Iran plant -- plan to assassinate President Trump. Does that explain the plane switcheroo and the renewed strikes by the U.S.?

Urgent work continues to save that buckling building in New York from partial collapse. Is the timeline too ambitious?

And from hot girl summer to hot surveillance summer. Is Meta trying to sweep privacy concerns under the rug by making Kylie Jenner the face of its new A.I. glasses?

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: Do not buy the Kylie Jenner Meta glasses. Don't buy them. Point blank, period. They are manufacturing consent.

(END VIDEO CLIP) CORNISH: So, this is new and exclusive video just into CNN. It's showing the moment a month ago that Senator Mitch McConnell was loaded into an ambulance.

Now, his current health status remains in question.

Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and we want to start with the discussion about the former Senate leader's health.

CNN has exclusively obtained video of the moment that the Kentucky Republican was taken by ambulance from his home. This happened on June 14.

Neighbors saw the senator being loaded into an ambulance on a stretcher. Two ambulances, a fire truck, and Capitol police officers were also seen blocking off the street.

Now, the face of the person isn't visible in the video, but one neighbor told CNN that an eyewitness saw McConnell's face.

Now, this is new detail around McConnell's hospitalization, which has been a mystery so far. Many Republican lawmakers on Capitol Hill say they've been kept in the dark.

REP. BRETT GUTHRIE (R-KY): We haven't spoken directly. I've text with him, and I've talked to people with his office and his staff. And, you know, I don't know much more than what the public knows.

SEN. MARSHA BLACKBURN (R-TN): I have not had any conversations with him. I understand that some of my colleagues have. We wish him a speedy recovery.

REP. MARLIN STUTZMAN (R-IN): Yes. No, no, I have not heard from him. And I think it's a question that Republicans should be asking.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: Do you know that he's alive?

STUTZMAN: I don't. You know, just the things that I've heard and seen from some friends is that he's obviously not doing well, but don't know if he's alive or has passed away.

CORNISH: So, joining me now in the group chat, Elliot Williams, CNN legal analyst and former federal prosecutor; Terry Schilling, president of the American Principles Project; and Lulu Garcia-Navarro, CNN contributor and "New York Times" journalist.

One of the reasons why people are talking about this, political people are talking about this in a kind of ghoulish way, frankly, is that the Kentucky Senate vacancy law, meaning what do you have to do if suddenly a Senate seat is open?

Well, there's a special election. The governor has to call for it. And the seat has to be empty until a winner is certified.

So, the idea is that the clock is somehow ticking on if, suddenly. there's an open seat in Kentucky, whether it can be filled.

What I don't know is why Republicans are kind of leading the charge online, talking about this. This is Nancy Mace in a social media post: "If McConnell is in as bad a shape as Biden ever was, or worse, he needs to step aside. This charade can't continue. We can't demand of others what we won't demand of ourselves."

TERRY SCHILLING, PRESIDENT, AMERICAN PRINCIPLES PROJECT: I think this is all getting kind of crazy, frankly. I can speak a little bit to this personally.

When my dad was running for Congress in Iowa, Western Iowa, in 2020, he was diagnosed with stage four intestinal cancer. And we made the decision that, since it was an ongoing election, to come public and disclose everything.

The reality is, is that I think McConnell is doing fine. Scott Jennings has backed up that he's completely fine, and he's coherent.

CORNISH: But don't skip over that. You said, "We made the decision to be public."

SCHILLING: Because there was a pending election that voters were going to make a decision on.

But you look at what's happening in the Senate right now. They're not doing anything of substance. There's -- there's not going to be a SAVE Vote Act. It looks like they don't need his vote right now.

He's announced his retirement. He's being replaced by, most likely, Andy Barr in Kentucky. It will be exciting to see.

But I think this is much ado about nothing. I think we should let -- give him the grace and give him some privacy. He'll come out when he is -- when he feels comfortable.

ELLIOT WILLIAMS, CNN LEGAL ANALYST: Can I ask a question? If you're a resident of the state of Kentucky and need help getting a passport with the State Department, who do you call?

SCHILLING: You call Senator Mitch McConnell's office. You -- oh, please, the Senate staff. Everyone knows that the Senate staff is really where the magic happens and makes it all work.

WILLIAMS: I think. Well, no, no, but I just -- I just think the notion that, because there is not a major piece of --

CORNISH: Fighting legislation.

WILLIAMS: Fighting legislation that's a priority to Republicans, that somehow, the residents of the state of Kentucky aren't entitled to -- aren't entitled to know, No. 1, what the health status is of one of the most senior senators in the United States.

WILLIAMS: And two, the fact that there isn't an election somehow obviates the senator from one transparency or to doing his job.

CORNISH: There's probably a trust issue, too, because I think during -- when McConnell was a leader, he was someone who manipulated the calendar a lot, somebody who would delay votes for this reason or that reason, most famously, when it came to the Supreme Court.

Meghan McCain weighed in, as well. Another child of a politico, saying, "I also have a lot of regrets almost a decade since my dad's passing. My family should have forced him to step down the second he was diagnosed, which is why I'm now an advocate of age and term limits in politics. I also sincerely hope Senator McConnell, OK."

What struck me there was the word "forced."

LULU GARCIA-NAVARRO, CNN CONTRIBUTOR: Yes. This is two things. And you have the experience of having it be both a political matter and a personal matter.

Everyone, as a personal matter, has had to deal with aging parents who you have to take their car keys away and --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: -- and have that difficult conversation. They have to decide what they're going to do when they -- you know, if they're going to go into special facilities, et cetera, et cetera.

When it becomes more than that, it's when it's a political matter and not just about the election, but about transparency.

And I do take the point that, at this point, if -- if Mitch McConnell is well enough to be gabbing to Scott Jennings, he should be well enough to call in to CNN or to "The New York Times" or to FOX News or whatever, to -- to show that he's OK.

And the fact that he hasn't done that is quite -- quite frankly, leading to these conspiracies.

SCHILLING: If I could, because, again, I think there's a charitable way to read this, especially if you know Mitch McConnell.

He is a very strong man. He does not like being told what to do, especially by public pressure.

SCHILLING: He -- he works on his own time frame. And so, I think you've got to give it to him. Give him a few days, take it -- take a deep breath. CORNISH: But let me play for someone you pointed out this week who you

could describe very similarly, the former defense secretary, Lloyd Austin. When he went to the hospital for his treatment, it became quite the scandal for Republicans.

And in the end, he came out, more or less kind of apologized. And I want to play for you the lesson that he took away from that moment.

LLOYD AUSTIN, FORMER SECRETARY OF DEFENSE: I don't think it's news that I'm a pretty private guy. I never liked burdening others with my problems. It's just not my way.

But I've learned from this experience, taking this kind of job means losing some of the privacy that most of us expect. The American people have a right to know if their leaders are facing health challenges that might affect their ability to perform their duties, even temporarily.

CORNISH: "Even temporarily." Also notice, he said, the loss of privacy is part of the gig.

And I just want to put up some images here, in memoriam. These are lawmakers who have died while they were holding office since 2024. And you pointed out many of these names.

SCHILLING: A lot of Dems up there.

CORNISH: A lot of "D's."

WILLIAMS: Well, it's a lot of old people.

WILLIAMS: I mean, I think let's just -- We should also discuss the fact that the United States Senate is a gerontocracy. It is. Well, it is.

CORNISH: I think the reason why people are discussing is because the Biden thing --

CORNISH: -- was like a big wakeup call where everybody was questioning, and Republicans are still questioning.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Can we talk about the Trump thing? I mean, this is the other thing.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: We don't know what President Trump actually is suffering from. What are his swelling -- SCHILLING: What are you talking about?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Well, there's a lot of --

CORNISH: There's -- we have done many segments on the rumors about various health issues.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: About various health issues where doctors speculated, with has swollen ankles, you know, the continued bruising on his arms, like -- on his hands.

I mean, there are lots of questions about, when -- when people get to a certain age. I mean, let's be honest. I mean, he's 80. Mitch McConnell is -- how old is Mitch McConnell now?

SCHILLING: He should be in his 80s. I don't think --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes, he's in his 80s. I mean, obviously, Biden was also in his 80s. I mean, you start to have serious health concerns.

And my point about this, more broadly, is I think the more general point, which is about transparency and what do you owe the voting public about --

CORNISH: What do you owe?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: What do you owe the voting public about your health?

CORNISH: And here's a fact I owe all of you. He's 84.

SCHILLING: Eighty-four.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Thank you.

CORNISH: But I have to be honest. One of the things that happened with Biden and Trump was this idea of, like, well, there's a vigorous and, you know, still operating 80-something. And then there's, like, a deteriorating something.

And I think what the audience and voters want to know is, are we all on the same page about when it's OK not to say anything --

CORNISH: -- about what's happening? And the answer is sounding more and more like no.

WILLIAMS: Well, yes. And moreover, I think a lot of people showed their behinds about Bidens age. I mean, I think we went through -- people don't remember, there was a year-long debate about Joe Biden being unfit for the presidency.

WILLIAMS: Which was right for us to have as a nation, because it was clear the president was not fit.

This is an individual who is, No. 1, one of the senior leaders on the Appropriations Committee, a former Senate majority leader and a representative serving the state of Kentucky.

And I think it's a reasonable question to ask, is this individual fit to serve? And are all the people who are out there vouching for his health creating problems for themselves?

WILLIAMS: Later on, by being on the record about it?

CORNISH: Let me follow up on something else quite serious. Coming up on CNN THIS MORNING, is Iran looking to assassinate President Trump? This was the warning from Israel to tip off the White House.

Plus, extreme heat fueling dangerous and deadly wildfires in Spain.

And where will LeBron go? The odds on Kalshi say there's a greater than 60 percent chance he heads to Cleveland, followed by the Heat and Warriors. We're going to tell you what Charles Barkley thinks.

CHARLES BARKLEY, SPORTS ANALYST/FORMER PROFESSIONAL BASKETBALL PLAYER: He should go back to Cleveland and finish his career there. If he goes to Philly, there's people going to say he's ring-chasing to catch Michael. If he goes to Golden State, they're going to say he's ring- chasing.

CORNISH: So, an alleged plot by Iran to assassinate President Trump, uncovered by Israel. Sources tell CNN they shared the intelligence with the United States this week.

DONALD TRUMP, U.S. PRESIDENT: I'm No. 1 on the kill list for Iran. They're lovely people. I'm No. 1. I may be gone, too, because I'm their No. 1 target. It's out all over the place. I'm their No. 1, because they're scum.

CORNISH: Now, during funeral processions for the former Iranian leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, a hardline Iranian newspaper put a $100 million bounty on Trump's head.

And there were countless banners calling for the president to be assassinated, along with other U.S. politicos like Senator Lindsey Graham.

Graham posted this photo on social media, remarking, "At least they used a good photo of me."

Joining the group chat, Holly Dagres, senior fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy.

Obviously, the U.S. and Israel were able to take out whole tiers of leadership in Iran. So, talk about how serious this assassination threat is, could have been based on what you're reading on the sources.

HOLLY DAGRES, SENIOR FELLOW, WASHINGTON INSTITUTE FOR NEAR EAST POLICY: Well, first, we should start by saying that this was related to the targeted killing of Quds Force Commander Qasem Soleimani by a U.S. drone strike in Baghdad in 2020.

So, these calls for an assassination of not just President Donald Trump, but senior U.S. officials that were in the first Trump administration: then-Secretary of State Mike Pompeo, national security adviser John Bolton, among those. And actually, there have been two plots that were foiled related to the latter two.

So, this isn't entirely new. But to your point, I mean, this is right now happening after a three-month war.

CORNISH: I do want to raise something, which is that the people who have been very critical of this war and critical of the U.S. and the Netanyahu and Trump relationship, have kind of questioned intel from Netanyahu, for example, on nuclear breakout periods or something like that.

I want to play for you. This is Hillary Clinton. This is many weeks back, but she was talking about what it was like to deal with Netanyahu when she was secretary of state. And it's relevant here. Let me play it for you.

HILLARY CLINTON, FORMER U.S. SECRETARY OF STATE: I remember one day I was on the phone for hours with Ehud, with Bibi, with others, you know. And they would say things like, you know, our planes are on the tarmac.

And I'd say, Well, good luck. I mean, great. Why are you doing this?

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: You're saying you were -- they were -- you were -- you were being played?

CLINTON: All the time.

(END VIDEO CLIP) CORNISH: Can you talk about this in this context? I think where people are speaking about Netanyahu and Israel with suspicion.

DAGRES: Well, we've seen incidents where the Israeli prime minister, Benjamin Netanyahu, has been speaking to an audience of one, which famously in 2018, right before President Donald Trump withdrew from the Iran nuclear deal, Netanyahu did this very well-known presentation in English, of course, about new findings that Mossad had found from some files they had stolen from an undeclared site.

But even though it wasn't exactly new information, the fact that this had been presented was really what sold President Trump to decide that he was finally going to withdraw from the nuclear deal in May of 2018.

So, I think that a lot of questions are arising in this moment, because it's clear that the goals that the Israelis had for the war hadn't been met, but had to be deterred because of the ceasefire. And there's an understanding that there's still unfinished business with Tehran.

CORNISH: I know it's been actually hard for you, because you have a lot of family and connection to Iran. We've been seeing these images of people mourning the death of the supreme leader.

But this is a dictatorship with -- that had a severe and violent crackdown of protests. What is the feeling for people who didn't have to make their way out into those streets?

DAGRES: Well, you know, ironically, this past week marked the six- month anniversary of the January anti-regime uprising that resulted in the deaths of thousands of protesters. And so, a lot of those Iranians were honoring their memory, the people that could afford to in the capital of Tehran, where much of the main procession was happening for Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's death was -- people took it as an opportunity to leave, to go to the North or Caspian Sea.

And so, those people were not on the streets. They were not. And if you were an anti-regime protests or lost a loved one, I remember some of the commentary after the Internet blackout ended was, oh, well, you didn't let us bury our loved ones, but it took you many months to bury your own, meaning the supreme leader. So, I think that really tells a lot about this moment.

CORNISH: Are people still scared?

DAGRES: They're scared for many reasons, Audie. They're scared because they don't know where their future is, because they don't -- if you're an anti-regime Iranian, you don't want this regime.

And you had the United States arguably say that help was on the way. That is, President Donald Trump, and that this was a regime change, or at least initially.

And then to see that this is a more emboldened regime, one that, surprisingly, has more support from people that are critics of the war.

And -- but also there's a worry: like, what if this war does continue? Their lives have gotten effectively worse. Economy's in shambles. They're saying they haven't seen numbers like that since the Iran-Iraq war.

And so, if you're a young Iranian, you feel like your future -- there isn't really one. So, this is where Iranians are feeling, at least in this moment. I would say.

CORNISH: OK. Holly, thank you so much for talking to us in this inflection point. We'll have you back soon.

CORNISH: Now, after the break on CNN THIS MORNING, we're going to talk about the worker who saved a New York City high rise from collapse. They're speaking out.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: In this situation. They're calling me a hero. I don't believe I am.

CORNISH: The job isn't done yet, however. Can workers save the building from partial demolition?

Plus, Meta bolsters privacy features on its secret recording Ray-Ban glasses. Will that make them any less creepy?

And a live look at the White House. The president's next construction project, the White House columns. You can see already they are covered with scaffolding.

KYLIE JENNER, REALITY TV STAR: Hi, I'm Kylie Jenner, the new voice of Meta A.I. Hey, Meta A.I., is that a chicken?

CORNISH: OK, Kylie Jenner. As she says, the new face and, it turns out, voice of Meta A.I. glasses. But, as everything with the Kardashians and A.I., comes the backlash.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: There's a way for you to tell, usually, if somebody is filming on their phone. With the glasses, there is no way for you to tell. And they are trying to get women more and more comfortable with that.

So, enter Kylie Jenner with this very cute pair of glasses.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: This collaboration with Kylie ultimately is roping in women consumers.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: To me, it's almost as if Meta is saying, like, OK, these creepy men are recording you, but hey, you can record them right back.

CORNISH: All right. Needless to say, seems to be some real concerns. Turns out users can actually tamper with that LED light on the glasses, so you wouldn't know if you were being recorded.

So, now Meta is trying to address some of those concerns, saying it's rolling out a new update that will turn off the camera if users turn off the privacy light.

So, we're talking about all of this on this week's "Engagement Party." So, joining the group chat, Ari Shapiro, my co-host of "Engagement Party."

So, we were joking about the various tiers of people who hate this.

ARI SHAPIRO, CO-HOST, "ENGAGEMENT PARTY": Yes. This intersects with a lot of different backlashes.

SHAPIRO: All going on at once.

CORNISH: But also, there are some people who might make use of them. Help me understand why it's such a big thing online.

SHAPIRO: All right. Well, first of all, you've got the A.I. backlash. You've got the tech billionaires backlash. You've got the surveillance technology backlash.

So, this is kind of summed up by people who are online calling this either Cybertrucks for the face, or there was another line I saw that: was the biggest advance in pervert technology since the trench coat.

SHAPIRO: I think Meta has a public history of, No. 1, compromising their users' safety and security and privacy, then publicly apologizing for it.

And No. 2, sinking tens of billions of dollars into transformative technologies that then die, like the Metaverse.

Too early to say if this is going to be one or both of those things, but the public response is, to say the least, not positive. CORNISH: Yes. And we should say what started out kind of like social

media stuff here and there, especially being posted by women, has moved to the lawsuit face [SIC] -- phase of this with you've got some states -- let me see who it was. There's some kid safety lawsuits that they're dealing with, four state attorneys general seeking $1.4 trillion in damages.

And there have been a couple other cases, as you've mentioned, that Meta has either lost or settled this year. And then there's just, Lulu, people like you who have encountered these in the wild.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: I've encountered these in the wild. It was deeply uncomfortable. Someone was, I thought, filming me when I was with my daughter. I didn't like it. And then it just starts to --

CORNISH: And where were you?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: I was on a train.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: So, I was in a public space. But at the same time, it leads to these very awkward interactions.

What are the social niceties for me to say, like, hello, sir, I don't -- are you filming me with your Meta glasses? I don't want to be filmed."

SHAPIRO: Did he admit it?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: No. It was just like this incredibly awkward.

segments.title: CNN.com - Transcripts
segments.text:
Return to Transcripts main page

Mexico Seeks investigation of Migrant Deaths; Maggie Haberman is Interviewed about Her Book on Trump; Victor Marx Wins Primary in Colorado. Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired July 10, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

LULU GARCIA-NAVARRO, CNN CONTRIBUTOR: I don't -- are you filming me you're your Meta glasses? I don't want to be filmed.

ARI SHAPIRO, CO-HOST, "ENGAGEMENT PARTY" PODCAST: Did he admit it?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: No. It was just like this incredibly awkward interaction where he just, like, kind of glared at me, took off his glasses, and, you know, it was just this very weird -- as a, you know, we gotcha.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Yes. Like, I'm not filming you, but I'm taking these off. Yes.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes. And so the whole thing was just really uncomfortable. And I don't, you know, and I don't know what the social interactions are. I put -- I posted this on social media. I got all these responses basically saying, it's legal to film people in most states, which I think you can speak to.

ELLIOT WILLIAMS, CNN LEGAL ANALYST: Yes.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: So, suck it up.

CORNISH: What's the deal? What can we do?

WILLIAMS: It really is legal to film people in most states. Think about every time you go to a monument or a park, you see someone out with their cell phone. Well, they're filming you too. You're incidentally in the camera.

Now, it gets trickier where you are recording one person. Some states you have to -- both people have to know that they're being -- that someone's being recorded.

WILLIAMS: Some states you can just record one person.

One interesting thing that Ari had said about Meta being sued or just taking fire over the years over its users privacy --

SHAPIRO: Especially for Instagram.

WILLIAMS: Especially for Instagram. Here -- this is interesting because it's not just users' privacy, it's third party's privacies. People like Lulu who are now at -- or anybody now at risk. It's not just the user who's getting pulled into this, right? And so, even if someone's not being filmed, there's just this specter of --

WILLIAMS: At any point I might be filmed, I don't know if I'm being filmed, and that's deeply uncomfortable.

WILLIAMS: To admit (ph) to people.

CORNISH: And we talked about the potential uses for this.

CORNISH: In the accessibility community. People who are hard of hearing. People who have issues with their site. The information they'd be able to get in the glasses.

I'm pretty sure I can guess lawmakers -- law enforcement is going to be excited about this. So, there are uses for it. And it's interesting that the way they're marketing it though is like, hey girls, this is fun.

SHAPIRO: Oh, they've got this lineup of mega celebs. I mean they're spending, I think, $70 billion to promote and produce these.

SHAPIRO: And they've got Teyana Taylor, Doja Cat, Kylie Jenner. Like, they are bringing out the all-stars to try to tell people that this is cool. I feel like it's part of Mark Zuckerberg's long campaign to convince people that he's cool. He wanted to be the Elon Musk pre- DOGE, and maybe his reputation is heading more in a direction of Elon Musk today.

CORNISH: Even if he's in the front row of Prada at the start of their relationship?

SHAPIRO: Yes, I'm not sure that that's a sign.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: But it's all -- but at the end of the day, nobody really trusts Meta with their own personal safety online.

CORNISH: The data that's ours.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: The data that's ours. I mean right now there's a whole kerfuffle over Instagram. If you have a public facing Instagram page, Meta is now automatically opting you in to basically allowing your public Instagram page to be manipulated by A.I. You have to actually actively go in there, which I did yesterday, to change those settings. These are the kinds of actions that make people very upset.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: CAA is getting involved in this, of course, the big Hollywood agency. These are issues that really come at the core of people's identity, their public facing profiles.

SHAPIRO: So, I think what we're seeing here, as I say in "Engagement Party," in our latest episode, is, number one, a failure of government to actually protect people's privacy and identity. But number two, a success of the people who are saying, you may be spending tens of billions of dollars to make this a thing, but we don't want it to be a thing.

SHAPIRO: And the people have the final say.

CORNISH: What do the young say, it's propaganda they're unsubscribing from?

Ari Shapiro, thank you so much.

You can catch us on the newest episode of "Engagement Party" now. New episodes are out Friday. We talk about this and also the movement of FROYO. And so, it's a lot of fun.

All right, straight ahead on CNN THIS MORNING, we're going to turn to this news. The threats from Mexico to take the U.S. to court over the deaths of ICE detainees. And could a foreign government succeed where U.S. advocates have failed?

Plus, personality over substance. Voters getting candidates who are actually qualified for the job. Maybe. Maybe not.

CORNISH: So, the 52-year-old Mexican man killed by ICE in Houston this week was not the target of an immigration operation. That's according to a source. Texas authorities previously notified ICE about two people believed to be in the U.S. illegally, traveling in a white van. And neither of those men were the victim.

You're looking at video from the aftermath of the shooting. I have to warn you, it is disturbing because you can see a bloodied person on the ground. Now, there's no official footage of the shooting that has been released. Federal officials involved were not wearing body cameras. ICE claims the man attempted to take off as agents tried to pull him over. His family is pushing back on that.

RONALDO SALGADO, SON OF ICE SHOOTING VICTIM: He did not know that those vehicles following him were ICE agents. Again, these vehicles were unmarked. They had no emblems, no logos, no flashing lights. There was no way for him to know that they were ICE agents. I know my dad would have stopped had ICE agents formally and clearly identified themselves, at least through their vehicle markings.

CORNISH: In the meantime, Mexico, fed up with the deaths of its people on U.S. soil. Their president is seeking a criminal investigation in the U.S. related deaths of 17 Mexican nationals during immigration operations in recent months.

PRESIDENT CLAUDIA SHEINBAUM, MEXICO (through translator): What happened recently with the death of a Mexican in an ICE operation, not only is it sad and regrettable, but it seems to be targeted. So, obviously, we will maintain the diplomatic relationship. But we made the decision to file a formal complaint with the state and federal prosecutor's office of the United States against those who are found responsible for what we consider to be homicides.

CORNISH: Mexico's foreign minister says the country will file criminal complaints directly with U.S. prosecutors.

The group chat is back.

And, Elliot, we need your expertise.

CORNISH: Because, in general, when you're looking at deaths of ICE detainees --

CORNISH: Obviously there is a spike. As of 2025, that number up to 33. It's gone down a bit. And I think people thought maybe ICE had been operating differently, or at least more covertly on the streets of the U.S.

CORNISH: You're shaking your head.

WILLIAMS: No. It's just covertly might be the word or just simply not in public in the same way. Now, the big picture governing all of this is that law enforcement has

tremendously broad latitude. And I think latitude that would shock and alarm many people, both in terms of deaths in custody, but also in terms of their conduct in the field.

Now, certainly, Mexico is free to try to, you know, engage prosecutors, file lawsuits or whatever else. I will note, you know, the Department of Homeland Security will be defended by the Justice Department in any federal suit in the United States right now. And I just think the likelihood of any of these suits really gaining traction --

WILLIAMS: Just based on who the Justice Department is right now, they have an interest in protecting the Department of Homeland Security, is pretty low, but they can. That is their right.

But two other avenues. Really quick, Audie, two other avenues for proceeding, if they're serious. Number one, go to the states. Every state can entertain lawsuits, even against the federal government. You can have the attorney general or local prosecutor bring a lawsuit. But also Congress can oversee and step in if ICE is behaving in a manner that's not appropriate.

CORNISH: So, right now, ICE related Mexican national deaths in Trump's term, 14 people in ICE custody, three in ICE operations.

Sheinbaum had more or less kind of played nice with Trump when it came to tariffs, right, and when it came to the drug war. Why do you think, Lulu, that she is speaking out now and in such a specific way, using the phrase targeted?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes. This is always a hugely sensitive issue for Mexico. Let's not forget that, you know, Mexico has the largest number of immigrants in the United States, and they share a border. And so, the way that ICE and Border Patrol interact with that population has always been a source of enormous sensitivity and tension. And I think one of the reasons they're doing this is not only because they think they're going to get satisfaction, but it is to be perceived by their own people as doing something, as raising complaints, as trying to actively engage.

Because it's not only that the government is in this very difficult position with trying to appease Trump and work with him while also defending its population, but the people themselves do not like the Trump administration, do not like the way that this is playing out, and want their government to do something.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And so, this is a way of them showing that they are.

CORNISH: And it raises the profile of ICE operations in a moment when they had effectively lowered the temperature after the mass protests in two separate cities, and deaths on the streets. TERRY SCHILLING, PRESIDENT, AMERICAN PRINCIPLES PROJECT: Yes. Well,

look, I -- my message to the president of Mexico, Claudia Sheinbaum, is, game on. You know, you want to sue over this. I guess we'll have to sue over the hundreds of thousands of young men and women in this country that have died from fentanyl overdoses or even the hundreds of people that have had violence inflicted on them that have been raped and sexually assaulted.

You know, Claudia Sheinbaum is one to talk. She was elected in a year where there were over 27 political assassinations in her party. She's literally a hand-picked candidate for the cartels. And again --

CORNISH: You've levied a lot of different things there.

CORNISH: So, I want to hold on because I'm not fact checking you on that level, OK. But I do have this question about, is it fair for a country to say, you're killing our nationals on your street and that this is state sanctioned deaths, and therefore.

SCHILLING: Well, look, I noticed in that chart, in 2023 and 2024 there were -- I think in 2023 it said there were seven deaths by ICE and 11 deaths in 2024. That's when there was like no immigration enforcement at all. And it's only around 50 between these last two years. There it goes, 33 and 19 in two years. That's 52.

SCHILLING: That's when we're actually deporting people and actually enforcing the law. So, it seems like there's some violence embedded into illegal immigration in the first place.

WILLIAMS: I -- well, no, no, no. I -- the only thing I will say to sort of push back on that gently is, and I worked at -- I was a senior official at ICE for five years. You're still talking about deporting 400,000 people a year, even under the low points of any administration's immigration enforcement efforts.

So, the idea that enforcement simply isn't happening, and those are, I believe we were talking about detention centers, people are detained, beds are full. It's just not accurate that no -- that no enforcement was happening.

Now, certainly we can agree, and I think we should, that more enforcement is happening now than happened in 2023. But I -- but I don't think that that -- those numbers are reflective of enforcement simply not happening in detention centers.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And it was --

WILLIAMS: So, a lot of people are detained by the United States.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And I'll also say that they -- (CROSS TALK)

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And I will say that the implication --

SCHILLING: And a lot more people were coming into the United States in those years.

WILLIAMS: Sure. Sure. Sure.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes, but I'll also say that the implication that somehow enforcement is violent and therefore these deaths are somehow acceptable is --

SCHILLING: Well, when --

CORNISH: And not accept -- well, let's pause on this. It's not acceptable, certainly, to the families. And as we started this story with somebody who's lost their father when they weren't even the target of the operation. So, I think the question of, what standard do we hold ourselves to is coming up in this.

I want you guys to stay with me. We're going to turn to another story in the future talking about NASA, because NASA is now recruiting volunteers for a yearlong moon and mars simulation. Voters will be in space-like -- sorry, volunteers will be in space-like conditions, living inside confined habitats and carrying out tasks, just as astronauts would at the start of the year.

CORNISH: An alleged plot by Iran to assassinate President Trump uncovered by Israel. Sources tell CNN they shared the intelligence with the U.S. this week.

I'm bringing in "New York Times" White House correspondent Maggie Haberman. She's co-author of the new bestseller "Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump."

Maggie, thank you so much for being here.

This seems like a good week to have this conversation between the inflection point ending the ceasefire and then this moment of this reporting we're getting now of a potential assassination attempt, information shared by Israel. This is a president who has survived assassination attempts. Does this kind of concern loom large for him?

MAGGIE HABERMAN, WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENT, "THE NEW YORK TIMES": Well, there's no question that among the reasons that the president and his team were really radicalized in the interregnum period before he came back to office, and we write about this in the book, were the assassination attempts that he faced. In particular, he -- Butler happened around the time that someone had been charged with being part of a murder for hire plot involving -- or set up by Iranian officials allegedly. And in his mind, and in the campaign's mind at the time, it all became one thing. And so, there's no question that the fact that this new information about a potential plot is going to loom large for this team. It is going to raise new concerns.

You are already seeing a president who, Audie, does not travel outside the White House that much. Part of it is that, you know, he would tell aides at the beginning of the -- of the term, I'm done campaigning. But part of it are -- is legitimate security concerns.

CORNISH: It's interesting talk -- hearing from you in this moment because he has such a different cast of characters around him compared to term one. How does that affect something like prosecuting this war, where we see like while people are having talks, he says, look, ceasefire is over, and all of a sudden we are in the middle of strikes again.

HABERMAN: So, there's a couple of things, Audie. One is that this government, unlike term one, and one of the things that we write about very clearly is how radically different this term is than the first term. The decisions are being made by a small handful of about a half a dozen advisors. And if someone is not in the room with those advisors in the government, they often have no idea what is going on. And that is generally by design, including Steve Witkoff, who is Trump's envoy for everything, who had been negotiating this ceasefire or some kind of an Iranian deal for some time.

But we also describe how last year Steve Witkoff thought that he was getting close to a deal, and then Trump made some other pronouncement, and it became very clear that foreign policy, as someone said, you know, and we quote them anonymously in the book, is, whatever Trump says at any given moment.

And so, you are seeing that right now with this ceasefire where I think it is -- it is most problematic is for diplomat -- long-term diplomatic conversations, incremental comments matter, mixed signals matter. And that has obviously happened for some time here. This is also a war that the president clearly wants to be done with. And there just is no quick fix. And that is frustrating him.

CORNISH: I want to turn to another issue because immigration has been obviously a defining issue for Trump in both terms, but arguably he's probably made the most gains in this term. Largely, Stephen Miller has looked at as the architect of that. Can you talk to me about the, where we are now, meaning Noem is gone, we were just talking about Mexico complaining about Mexican nationals being targeted. Is this something that the architects around Trump -- that it's by design?

HABERMAN: So, we have seen a lot of fits and starts with immigration efforts and policy because there was such a flood the zone effort. Stephen Miller spent several years preparing for a second Trump term. He looked at all of the levers of government. He had it -- he has an actual plan for what he wanted to do. He is more hard line on immigration than Trump is, although Trump obviously only tends to react and, you know, reel Miller in when there's negative media coverage. And so, you saw that earlier this year with the shooting deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti. And these showy ICE sweeps into cities, these, you know, dramatic

displays that the administration would clip and put out in social media videos, those slowed down, but that doesn't mean that the efforts slowed down. And so, you are going -- you are seeing -- my colleagues have written about this at "The Times," larger sweeps. You are seeing -- they're just broadcast with less vigor and intensity. It calmed the protests down at the time. I don't know where this goes now. But they are trying now -- Stephen Miller had a target of 3,000 arrests a day. That was not doable. Now there is a smaller target, but it's still quite high. And he is very intent on pushing this ahead for as long as he can.

CORNISH: OK, that's Maggie Haberman. Her new book with Jonathan Swan is called "Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump." It's out now.

I also want to turn to this, the ballot for governor in Colorado, it's set. Marine veteran Victor Marx secured the top Republican spot when the race was called last night. Both of his GOP rivals in the race have said they won't support him in the general election, calling him, quote, "a disaster" and a "con man."

VICTOR MARX (R), COLORADO CANDIDATE FOR GOVERNOR: So, if you are frustrated, skeptical or wondering if anything can actually change, I just proved it by winning the primary.

CORNISH: OK, group chat is back.

This is a coda. I'm sorry, Terry, this is a coda.

SCHILLING: You got me (ph).

CORNISH: You and I talked about this in -- last week. And you were like, look, ignore people like this. This was in our Platner discussion.

CORNISH: And you were like, look, this is not a guy that you should be focusing on. He is now the GOP candidate for governor in Colorado.

SCHILLING: In Colorado, in Illinois, in New York, in California.

CORNISH: But also two Republicans in the race.

CORNISH: Sour grapes or them literally being like, this disaster is a con man. SCHILLING: Again -- look, again, I think one of the biggest travesties

is when these blue states become so blue that they're not competitive. And when they're not competitive, you don't get the quality candidates that you need actually to win.

CORNISH: No, no, no, Terry, he raised $3 million. That's not ate (ph) money from Democrats.

SCHILLING: I know. That's not very much for -- but for a governor race --

SCHILLING: You know, you can raise that just by going on a few punchy podcasts. It's really not that much money for even a congressional race at this point.

CORNISH: Right. He claims to have rescued 45,000 abuse victims. He performs prayers to free people of demonic oppression. Claims to have called an airstrike on ISIS. Says he was forced to kill a man at age seven. Who among us?

SCHILLING: Well, look, we got Abigail Spanberger, who's an admitted CIA operative, as the governor of Virginia. I'd like to know what she's done in her career.

CORNISH: From the CIA?

SCHILLING: From the -- the CIA does some pretty gnarly stuff.

CORNISH: And you don't want to know about demonic -- OK. OK.

SCHILLING: No, I definitely want to know about that. I believe in the spiritual world.

CORNISH: I mean, work with me, Terry. Work with me.

Lulu, the reason why I'm talking about this is because candidate quality, a term that we heard from Mitch McConnell.

WILLIAMS: All week. Yes.

CORNISH: You know what I mean? It's been so on the minds of everyone with the Graham Platner thing in the Senate. And just like in Republican circles, in Colorado, the voters want what the voters want.

What have we learned about the limits of that?

GARCIA-NAVARRO: We've learned that demonic possession is a thing, clearly.

SCHILLING: Yes, 100 percent.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: That is -- that is -- that is --

CORNISH: Also teleporting to a Waffle House. A different story. SCHILLING: That's different.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And teleporting to a Waffle House. I mean -- listen, this is a huge issue, I think, for both Republicans and Democrats and what I think any -- or what I hear from both my Democratic and Republican friends who are in the political system in that way is that they say, well, it's because the parties are so weak and we no longer get to decide about quality candidates -- you know, candidate quality. These decisions used to be made in backrooms. And so we could really vet people and put the best candidate forward.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: And that is true.

What voters will say is, yay, I'm so glad that this is no longer being done in backrooms.

CORNISH: I know, but how come some people can ride it out and some people can't? Now, Platner is accused of violence. I think that clearly turned out to be a red line. But there's lots of other people dealing with scandals. Ken Paxton in Texas.

CORNISH: Obviously, Marx. I mean his -- one of the people he ran against said she ran a good race and, quote, "for the record, I still haven't killed anyone." It seems as though --

WILLIAMS: That's the bar.

CORNISH: Yes, that's the bar.

WILLIAMS: Well, but my point -- sure, but all I'm saying is that when the most activist people are the ones casting the votes, you're going to occasionally get stuff like this in --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: It's not only that --

GARCIA-NAVARRO: But to your point, actually, when you have one party rule in a state, the opposition party becomes very radicalized I think. We see that in California with the GOP. We see that, you know, in Democratic politics and sort of right leaning states. And so, I do think that you get like the most hardcore people putting candidates forward. GARCIA-NAVARRO: Yes. But maybe. But maybe not.

GARCIA-NAVARRO: I mean, I think everyone's trying their game and they're trying to see how far they can push it until voters or the parties or people just say, the establishment says enough is enough.

sentences.en: CNN has exclusively obtained video of the moment that the Kentucky Republican was taken by ambulance from his home.
sentences.cn: CNN独家获得了肯塔基州共和党人（麦康奈尔）被救护车从家中接走的视频。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'CNN has exclusively obtained video'，'of the moment'修饰video，'that'引导定语从句修饰'the moment'，从句中主语为'the Kentucky Republican'，谓语为'was taken'。

sentences.en: One of the reasons why people are talking about this, political people are talking about this in a kind of ghoulish way, frankly, is that the Kentucky Senate vacancy law, meaning what do you have to do if suddenly a Senate seat is open?
sentences.cn: 人们谈论此事的原因之一，坦率地说，政治人物以一种病态的方式谈论此事，是因为肯塔基州参议员空缺法，即如果参议员席位突然空缺，你必须做什么？
sentences.structure: 主系表结构 + 同位语从句
sentences.analysis: 主语为'One of the reasons'，'why'引导定语从句修饰'reasons'，主句谓语为'is'，表语为'that'引导的从句，其中'meaning'后为解释说明。

sentences.en: And I think it's a reasonable question to ask, is this individual fit to serve?
sentences.cn: 我认为问这个问题是合理的：这个人是否适合任职？
sentences.structure: 主句 + 宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I think'，后接省略that的宾语从句，从句中'it'为形式主语，真正主语为'to ask'，后接直接引语疑问句。

sentences.en: And are all the people who are out there vouching for his health creating problems for themselves later on, by being on the record about it?
sentences.cn: 所有那些公开为他健康担保的人，日后是否会因为公开表态而给自己制造麻烦？
sentences.structure: 主句 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'are all the people...creating problems'，'who'引导定语从句修饰'people'，'by being on the record'为方式状语。

sentences.en: But even though it wasn't exactly new information, the fact that this had been presented was really what sold President Trump to decide that he was finally going to withdraw from the nuclear deal in May of 2018.
sentences.cn: 但尽管这并非全新信息，但这一呈现方式实际上说服了特朗普总统最终决定在2018年5月退出核协议。
sentences.structure: 让步状语从句 + 主句 + 表语从句
sentences.analysis: 'even though'引导让步状语从句，主句主语为'the fact'，后接同位语从句，谓语为'was'，表语为'what'引导的从句，其中包含'that'引导的宾语从句。

sentences.en: And to see that this is a more emboldened regime, one that, surprisingly, has more support from people that are critics of the war.
sentences.cn: 而看到的是一个更加胆大的政权，令人惊讶的是，它得到了更多战争批评者的支持。
sentences.structure: 不定式短语 + 同位语 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 'to see that'为不定式短语，'that'引导宾语从句，'one'为同位语，后接'that'引导的定语从句，其中又包含'that'引导的定语从句修饰'people'。

sentences.en: And if you were an anti-regime protests or lost a loved one, I remember some of the commentary after the Internet blackout ended was, oh, well, you didn't let us bury our loved ones, but it took you many months to bury your own, meaning the supreme leader.
sentences.cn: 如果你曾是反政府抗议者或失去了亲人，我记得网络中断结束后的一些评论是：哦，你们不让我们埋葬亲人，但你们却花了几个月才埋葬自己的（最高领袖）。
sentences.structure: 条件状语从句 + 主句 + 宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 'if'引导条件状语从句，主句为'I remember'，后接省略that的宾语从句，从句主语为'some of the commentary'，谓语为'was'，后接直接引语，'meaning'为现在分词作状语。

sentences.en: And there were countless banners calling for the president to be assassinated, along with other U.S. politicos like Senator Lindsey Graham.
sentences.cn: 还有无数横幅呼吁暗杀总统，以及其他美国政客，如参议员林赛·格雷厄姆。
sentences.structure: there be句型 + 现在分词短语
sentences.analysis: 'there were'为存在句型，主语为'countless banners'，'calling for'为现在分词短语作定语修饰'banners'，'along with'为介词短语。

sentences.en: So, this is new and exclusive video just into CNN, showing the moment a month ago that Senator Mitch McConnell was loaded into an ambulance.
sentences.cn: 所以，这是CNN刚刚获得的独家新视频，显示一个月前参议员米奇·麦康奈尔被抬上救护车的时刻。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 现在分词短语 + 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'this is video'，'showing'为现在分词短语作定语，'that'引导定语从句修饰'the moment'。

sentences.en: And I think it's a reasonable question to ask, is this individual fit to serve?
sentences.cn: 我认为问这个问题是合理的：这个人是否适合任职？
sentences.structure: 主句 + 宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I think'，后接省略that的宾语从句，从句中'it'为形式主语，真正主语为'to ask'，后接直接引语疑问句。

sentences.en: I think one of the reasons they're doing this is not only because they think they're going to get satisfaction, but it is to be perceived by their own people as doing something, as raising complaints, as trying to actively engage.
sentences.cn: 我认为他们这样做的原因之一，不仅是因为他们认为会得到满意结果，而且是为了让本国人民看到他们在采取行动、提出抗议、积极介入。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 表语从句（one of the reasons...is not only...but...），其中not only...but连接两个并列的表语从句，but后的it is to be perceived...包含三个并列的as短语作主语补足语。
sentences.analysis: 该句结构复杂，包含多个从句和并列结构。not only...but...强调双重目的，as doing something等三个短语具体说明被感知的方式。

sentences.en: And I think one of the reasons they're doing this is not only because they think they're going to get satisfaction, but it is to be perceived by their own people as doing something, as raising complaints, as trying to actively engage.
sentences.cn: 我认为他们这样做的原因之一，不仅是因为他们认为会得到满意结果，而且是为了让本国人民看到他们在采取行动、提出抗议、积极介入。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 表语从句（one of the reasons...is not only...but...），其中not only...but连接两个并列的表语从句，but后的it is to be perceived...包含三个并列的as短语作主语补足语。
sentences.analysis: 该句结构复杂，包含多个从句和并列结构。not only...but...强调双重目的，as doing something等三个短语具体说明被感知的方式。

sentences.en: But we also describe how last year Steve Witkoff thought that he was getting close to a deal, and then Trump made some other pronouncement, and it became very clear that foreign policy, as someone said, you know, and we quote them anonymously in the book, is, whatever Trump says at any given moment.
sentences.cn: 但我们也描述了去年史蒂夫·维特科夫如何认为他即将达成协议，然后特朗普发表了其他声明，结果变得非常清楚：外交政策，正如有人所说——我们在书中匿名引用了他们——就是特朗普在任何时刻所说的任何话。
sentences.structure: 主句（we describe）+ 宾语从句（how...），宾语从句中包含三个并列分句（thought...，made...，became...），最后一个分句中包含that引导的主语从句（that foreign policy...is...），其中插入语as someone said和we quote them。
sentences.analysis: 该句通过how引导的宾语从句叙述事件发展，并列结构展示因果链。插入语as someone said和we quote them增加了口语化和引用色彩。

sentences.en: And so, you are seeing that right now with this ceasefire where I think it is -- it is most problematic is for diplomat -- long-term diplomatic conversations, incremental comments matter, mixed signals matter.
sentences.cn: 所以，你现在在停火问题上看到的就是这种情况，我认为最成问题的是外交官——长期外交对话中，逐步的评论很重要，混杂的信号很重要。
sentences.structure: 主句（you are seeing that）+ 宾语从句（where I think...），其中I think后接省略that的从句，从句中it is most problematic is for diplomat...，后跟两个并列名词短语（incremental comments matter, mixed signals matter）作解释。
sentences.analysis: 该句口语化特征明显，where引导的定语从句修饰ceasefire，I think插入表示观点，破折号表示停顿和修正。

topics.title: Mitch McConnell's Health and Transparency
topics.content: 美国参议员米奇·麦康奈尔（Mitch McConnell）的健康状况引发关注。CNN独家视频显示他一个月前被救护车接走，但官方未公开详情。共和党内部对透明度有质疑，部分议员表示不知情。肯塔基州参议员空缺法规定，若席位空缺需举行特别选举。此事引发关于政客健康透明度的讨论，类似此前拜登和特朗普的健康争议。
topics.keywords: Mitch McConnell, health, transparency, Senate vacancy, special election

topics.title: Iran Assassination Plot Against Trump
topics.content: 以色列向美国提供情报，称伊朗计划暗杀特朗普总统。此前伊朗强硬派报纸曾悬赏1亿美元刺杀特朗普。美国与以色列在伊朗核问题及地区冲突中合作密切，但以色列情报的可信度曾受质疑，如内塔尼亚胡关于伊朗核突破期的说法。伊朗国内反政府情绪与对未来的担忧并存。
topics.keywords: Iran, assassination plot, Trump, Israel, intelligence, nuclear deal

topics.title: Meta's AI Glasses and Privacy Backlash
topics.content: Meta与凯莉·詹娜（Kylie Jenner）合作推广其AI眼镜，引发隐私担忧。用户可篡改LED指示灯，使他人无法察觉被录制。Meta随后更新软件，关闭摄像头时自动关闭指示灯。公众批评该技术为“脸上的赛博卡车”或“变态技术”，并担忧其被滥用于偷拍。Meta此前因隐私问题多次被起诉。
topics.keywords: Meta, AI glasses, privacy, Kylie Jenner, surveillance, backlash

topics.title: Meta智能眼镜与隐私争议
topics.content: Meta推出智能眼镜，具备拍摄功能，引发隐私担忧。CNN主持人Lulu Garcia-Navarro在节目中遭遇他人用Meta眼镜拍摄，讨论公共场合拍摄的合法性及Meta在隐私方面的不良记录。
topics.keywords: Meta, smart glasses, privacy, filming, public space

topics.title: 墨西哥就ICE致死事件向美国施压
topics.content: 一名52岁墨西哥男子在休斯顿被ICE执法人员射杀，引发墨西哥政府强烈反应。墨西哥总统Sheinbaum要求美国进行刑事调查，并指控ICE行动导致17名墨西哥人死亡。讨论涉及ICE执法透明度、墨西哥外交策略及美国国内政治。
topics.keywords: ICE, Mexico, migrant deaths, criminal investigation, diplomatic tension

topics.title: 特朗普政府移民政策与ICE执法
topics.content: 特朗普第二任期移民执法力度加大，ICE逮捕人数上升，但执法方式引发争议。Stephen Miller作为政策设计者，推动大规模逮捕，但面临执行困难。墨西哥死亡事件凸显执法中的暴力问题。
topics.keywords: Trump, immigration, ICE, Stephen Miller, enforcement

topics.title: 伊朗涉嫌暗杀特朗普阴谋
topics.content: 以色列情报显示伊朗计划暗杀特朗普，已与美国分享情报。特朗普此前曾遭遇暗杀未遂，此事可能加剧其安全担忧，并影响外交决策。
topics.keywords: Iran, assassination plot, Trump, Israel, intelligence

topics.title: 特朗普新书《政权更迭》内容
topics.content: 《纽约时报》记者Maggie Haberman新书《政权更迭：特朗普的帝王式总统》出版，揭示特朗普第二任期决策过程、外交政策混乱及移民政策幕后。
topics.keywords: Maggie Haberman, Regime Change, Trump, book, White House

topics.title: 科罗拉多州长共和党初选结果
topics.content: 海军老兵Victor Marx赢得科罗拉多州长共和党初选，但其对手拒绝支持，称其为“灾难”和“骗子”。讨论候选人质量问题，以及极端候选人在深蓝州的崛起。
topics.keywords: Colorado, governor, primary, Victor Marx, candidate quality
